speaker: interviewer
text: 5 Of Max Eisen. Please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: So here we are sort of fooling around and talking to each other. And all of the sudden, I see my partner, who is working with me, who is facing me, because we are, sort of, shoveling from opposite each other, he starts to work fast. And that gave me a signal that somebody is coming or somebody is nearby.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, and I start to work myself. But I kind of felt something hot on my neck, you know, and I put my hand there, and I see I've got blood on my hand. So as I'm leaning down, shoveling away, I can see blood dripping down.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I look back behind me and there's an SS soldier behind me. And he hit me with his gun butt right on my head, over here. I still have a bend, here. And you know, I felt no pain. It's funny, when you get hit on the head, there is no pain. It was just a buzzing kind of a feeling happening, and blood was really coming hard. And I was more scared than anything else, you know. And I'm working away, and this blood is dripping. And finally, I just passed out. I just fell down, and I guess, more from shock. And I remember this [INAUDIBLE]. He was a Polish fellow. Stan was his name. And he, they tried to stop the bleeding. And he tore off a piece from my jacket, and he told me to urinate on it, and he put this on this wound, and it stopped the bleeding. I guess it was ammonia or something, you know. And I was thrown into a ditch for the rest of the day. I couldn't stand up, because I lost too much blood. I was too weak, And a [INAUDIBLE], I remember, was walking by, and he showed me this sign. And you know, this sign meant that you're going up in smoke.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I sort of accepted this, that at the end of the day when we were going back there, they would just drop me off, and-- near the crematoriums, you know. So anyway, I was-- at the end of the day, two guys had to drag me back on my rear end. Each one of-- each of them was pulling me by one leg, and they pulled me like that all the way back to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took me to the operating room, and the doctors operated on me. They stitched me up, they cleaned up my head, and I was put into a bed. I guess this was at the beginning of the week. And came Friday, I was no, not much better. I could not walk. I was just too weak. And in hospital, they gave you a bowl of soup a day, but it was made from cream of wheat. It was like a white substance, like cream of wheat. That's the best I can describe it. And you were in a bed all day, and the doctors came, and they changed this bandage on me. There was a French doctor, a Jewish doctor that did this every day. And usually every Friday, the camp doctor, the [INAUDIBLE], who was a SS high-ranking captain. He was a [INAUDIBLE] doctor. He came with-- and a truck stood outside in front of the hospital, and some SS orderlies came, and all the people from the hospital were taken on stretchers. Those that couldn't walk, they were just dumped on the truck and taken away to Birkenau to the gas chambers. And here I was. You know, I was in this group, too. And this French doctor came, and he yanked me out of bed. He grabbed my card from the bed, because you know, they had a card on the bed, and you know, they just collected the cards, and bingo, you were gone. And he took me out, and he took me downstairs to the operating room. And he gave me a white smock, and he said that you will be cleaning up the operating room. And I didn't say anything. I was very thankful, you know, that he did this to me. I realized what he did. But you know, he had no-- had they taken me, I would have been gone. I couldn't resist this, you know. So here I was in the operating room. I became the cleaner. I was cleaning the floors. I was cleaning the walls with disinfectant, and I was 15 years old. And I kept this place in tip top shape, you know. I really enjoyed doing what I was doing there. You know, it gave me a new lease on life. The first thing that happened to me that I was able to clean my clothes, because after every operation, I was taking all the bloody gowns and sheets and everything to the laundry, you know, to clean. And once you had a position like that-- so I sort of-- my level sort of went up, too. You know, I became sort of a-- not quite a prominent, you know, but I was an assistant in the operating room, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: What benefits did that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: --bring you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was able to go to the wascherei, for instance, and get some clean clothes, you see, because there were clothes there that were cleaned that were shipped back to Germany all the time, you know, from transports, you see. So I had some underwear, you know, I had some shirts, and I had better stuff, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this was, you know-- you couldn't do this otherwise. You could never walk in. But here I am, an official. I'm coming from the operating room with all these gowns, you know, and I'm doing a service. So I was able to-- and I was able to, if I had some pills, I was able to barter with some pills, you know, to get some food, you see. So I'm cleaning, I'm sterilizing instruments, and you know, I was eventually preparing people for their operations, you know, like, shaving, you know, the people, and putting the gowns on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And most of the operations were a lot of hernia operations, here, and many bad accidents, people that were badly hurt on the job, or from shooting, and things like that. We had, we had ether. You know, we had novocaine to put people out. And they were usually, if they operated on a hernia or a lower part, they were giving them spinal. And we had no blood plasma, but we had instruments. We had a, a x-ray machine there. The doctors that were operating were two Polish doctors, gentile doctors. And I remember one name, a Dr. [? Orzeszko. ?] [? Orzeszko, ?] I think it means a nut. And he was the chief surgeon. He was a Polish political prisoner. And his assistant was a Polish political prisoner. And you know, they used to get parcels from home. And the French doctor was a Jewish doctor. He was an assistant, as well. And how my life improved while there, I was-- since I was the assistant, I was doing the cooking for the doctors at night. I mean, these doctors were not eating the camp fare, you know. Like, they had bread, and they had salamis, you know, and I had a big pot. And I had potatoes, you know, stored away, and I used to make a meal after the operations finished, about 7:00. We used to operate from about 12:00 to 7:00 every day. And I used to make a stew in this big pot, you know, with potatoes and salami and whatever I had. I just dumped it in. And I put it in this big sterilizer that we used in the operating room to-- after I picked up the gowns from the laundry room, I used to roll up the gowns and the masks, you know, into little balls and fit it into a drum. And these drums were stacked, then, into the pressure-- this big pressure cooker, you know. It was a boiler, kind of. You know, it was about that high. And I used to close the top of the boiler and just let on the steam, and this is the way I was making the meals, you see. It was like a pressure cooker, you see.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was cleaning the pots after, you know, and I had my own portion, you see. So this-- I got there, you know-- I mean, I could have been killed by this SS, you know. But this actually saved my life, because it sort of helped me come back to-- I picked up a lot of weight.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was unfortunate that my father and my uncle were gone by that time. You know, I couldn't help them any, and they helped me. To get to that stage, you know, and here I was, and I couldn't help them anymore, you see, that was the sad part. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Give me an idea of the layout and the facilities in the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, to do that, I bought a book. There was a Beate Klarsfeld, here. She is a German woman who put a book together. It's the plans of Auschwitz. And I bought this book, you know, just because I knew about her. She's an Nazi fighter, you know. She's a German, Christian woman. And when I bought this book, I was going through the pages, and there was Auschwitz and the operating room. And I said, this is the place where I was working. So in this book, you can see exactly how this operating room looked. OK. There was one room. The first room was-- it was a big room. That's where the sinks were, you know, where the doctors used to wash up. And I had all the instruments in cabinets, and the sterilizers were there. And there were credenzas with medicine and ampoules, you know, for the needles. And in the corner was an x-ray machine. And there were swinging doors going into the operating room. In the operating room was the table, the operating table, and the lamps on top. And this was it, mainly, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many operations were done, you know, daily?

speaker: interviewee
text: How many operations? Well, we used to operate-- it depends, you know. Lots of hernias, you know, used to take an hour, you know, that's it. And, you know, we just worked like machines, you know, one after the other, you know. There were many complicated operations. Sometimes-- I remember one operation, there was a Jewish person, and I think it was a scientist or somebody. But I knew he was a very important person. And he was brought by an SS ambulance. His head was not shaved, and he was in civilian clothes. I don't remember exactly what the operation was, but I know we had to work over above the heart. And they had to cut out some ribs, you know, from the ribs. I don't know whether it was TB of the ribs, or something. I don't know. But I remember we ran into a problem with this operation. I remember when they cut the ribs out, I could see the heart, you know, moving. But they could not catch some of the veins, and he was bleeding so bad. There were pails, you know, of the tampons, you know, that gauze, you know, that soaks up the blood. They had to-- I had to go and get somebody to give a body-to-body blood transfusion. You know, we didn't know blood type, because we had no facilities to do that. And I'm sure the doctors knew about blood typing, but I didn't understand this. All I know is that I got ahold of somebody. He was promised bread and salami. And I got ahold of a young man, and he was put on a bed right beside this person. And he was, they got it into his vein with a needle and into the patient. And he was given a direct transfusion. It was a controlled transfusion. You know, you could not give the blood fast, so they had to control the speed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remember this. And there were many-- this patient, you know, after we operated on him, he was put back into the SS ambulance, and he was taken away. I don't know. I never heard from him again. I mean, at least, I don't know where they took him.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had some very-- some shootings, that people were shot with a machine gun, and their bones were shattered, you know. So I don't know, do you-- would I-- have to describe this to you, you know. But this bone was shattered, and they had to cut it with a saw, and with plates, put together, so his leg will not be shorter, and that type of thing. And sometimes, early November, when the Americans were bombing, you know, like, we had many bombing raids over Auschwitz. I don't know, there must have been 1,000 planes coming overhead, and they were trying to bomb the factories all around. And one day, they knocked out the SS hospital. And so here we are one day, all of the sudden, two SS ambulances come in with the sirens to Auschwitz, and they come to the, to our operating room. And they are bringing in-- we have to get the patients off the table. I had to clean, disinfect everything, you know. And I remember several of them were in the hallways, these SS men. They were hit by shrapnel from the bombing. And we had to operate on them. You know, they were-- bones were-- you know, gashes in the heads, and all kinds of shrapnel wounds, you know. And we had to operate on them, but when we operated on these SS doctors, the [INAUDIBLE] the [INAUDIBLE] doctor, who was a surgeon himself, he was operating with the two Polish doctors, together, because they would not trust, you know, just the Poles to operate on them, you know. So-- and I was doing the rest of the, you know, sterilizing, getting the instruments ready, and so on. And after that, for about a week or two, they were bringing SS people, you know, to our hospital, and we had to give them preference over anybody else.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's the way things went on. You know, I remember other operations when people had very bad gangrene set in, in, in their legs, because you know, you have to understand, people were afraid to come to the hospital, because they knew, if you come to the hospital with a serious thing, and-- you never get out. So there are many people that had very bad injuries. And, you know, many people had very bad injuries on the back of their legs from the bad-- these clogs, the wooden clogs, you know? And they had very bad infections and gangrene, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember many times, you know, when they had to be amputated. And I took these limbs into the next block, where they were doing experiments, you know. I know that's where Dr. Mengele was operating, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had to take some of these limbs into the next block and just drop them into a vat, you know, and there were other things floating in there, you know, all kinds of body parts, and stuff like that. What kinds of things did you see in, in this other block?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see anything. I just saw young kids who were sort of almost dead, you know. Many twins, I saw there. Many young kids that-- they were castrated, you know. I saw them. And I saw Mengele a few times. He came into our operating room. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What would he be doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he just came. He was talking with the doctors. And I had no business to hear it, you know, and so I really don't know. And I remember other things. On every Friday-- you know, we had many SS-- these orderlies, you know, lower-ranking guys, like sergeants, and under-- you know, under sergeants, who were hanging around our operating room all the time. And we had to be very careful, because sometimes they brought some women from Birkenau, and these were some women that got pregnant. And they wanted to save their life, so they said that they had-- not hernia, but you know, when you have a [INAUDIBLE], when you have an intestine burst on you-- appendectomy, appendectomy, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they brought them in with an appendectomy, you know. And they actually performed abortions, and we had to be very careful, because if they found this out, you know, we would have all been shot, you know. So we had-- these soldiers were hanging around, because there was a different atmosphere, you know. And if they needed something to look after, you know, our doctors did that. And every Friday, I remember, when they were taking these sick-- you know, the people who were cleared out of the hospital on the truck to Birkenau, these SS soldiers went with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember when they came back, they were all comparing, you know, how many gold crowns each had-- you know, with teeth on it, you know. And I knew what was happening, you know. They went through the gas chambers, and before they were cremated, you know, they were-- their teeth were pulled out, you know. And you know, so everybody was profiting from this place, you know. This is-- there were thousands of Nazis that were-- this is the way they were making a lot of money, you know-- gold and whatever was found there. So I was working here till January of 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say the atmosphere was different in the hospital--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I mean, we were clean, you know, and these SSs, they needed some pills for anything, the doctors always gave it to them, you see. We had certain amount of pills.

speaker: interviewer
text: What types?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember. I couldn't tell you what, what we had, because-- you know, this I can't tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: What could pills be exchanged for?

speaker: interviewee
text: What could which?

speaker: interviewer
text: What could pills be exchanged for amongst the prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, for anything. For food, or for-- food, cigarettes, or what-- you know, anything of that sort. So I was there till January of-- January the 20th, 1945. They, on the 20th, that night-- prior to the 20th, several thousands of people were driven out of Auschwitz by the SS. They were taken away on transports somewhere else, and we didn't know where. And they were burning the cards. You know, they had thousands and thousands, hundreds of thousands of cards, because the Germans were chronic-- you know, they were very accurate. They recorded everybody that was tattooed, you know. They had them on a card to take-- you know, on a card system-- you know, where you came from-- so mine, with many others that were given-- tattooed numbers. And they were destroying all these hundreds of thousands of cards in January. It was-- they had a big pile of it dumped in Auschwitz, and they were pouring gasoline on it. And they were burning this. This, this was burning for days, because, you know, all this compressed paper, you know, did not burn too well. So somebody was tending it and kept pouring stuff on it. And you know, the crematoriums were all blown up in November. So there were no more gassing and cremation, because they did not want to leave any evidence behind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness the destruction of the crematorium?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I did not. I was in Auschwitz, and that was in Birkenau. I was in Auschwitz I, and that was in Birkenau. So the transports were leaving. Two transports have left, and they were gathering all the people from the satellite camps into Auschwitz, to where I was. And I recognized some, a couple of my friends from my town. They were brought in from a place called [INAUDIBLE]. They were working in coal mines. They were all black from this coal dust, and they were just sick. They couldn't walk anymore. They were laying down in this barrack. And I told them, look, you have to get up. And I was getting ready to go with this transport, because I had to make a choice whether I'm going to stay or whether I'm going to leave, you know. I knew that before the SS leave, they'll blow up the whole camp, or they'll shoot everybody, you know. I was, I didn't want to take that chance to stay on. And these people, they couldn't walk. And I was in, not in such a bad shape, you know, at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they said no, we're not, we're staying, you know. And so I went on this death march-- so-called death march. I found out since then that over 200,000 Jewish people were killed on these death marches from all the other camps, and including Auschwitz-- you know, that we were being marched west, you know. So here we are that night. I remember the artillery-- the Red Army was just miles away, and the artillery barrage, you know, and flashes of light, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember these [? Stalin ?] candles, they were dropping from planes, you know, that lit up the sky, you know. It was really eerie. And we were marching, you know, out in this forest and fields, you know, for about six days, day and night, nonstop.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably about 10,000 people. The column, you couldn't see the end or the beginning of it, you know? And I know that I was trying to get to the front of the column, you know, because if you got straggled, you know, then you were in bad shape. They would--

speaker: interviewer
text: So if you were in the front of the column, you, sort of, didn't have to walk as fast as if you were in the back, you know. Something, I don't know why. I had to go to the front of the column.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were walking five abreast. We had to hook our arms together, and whoever-- we slept, and we walked like zombies, you know. It was like, you know, you walked like a machine. And they didn't give us any food. I know I had a piece of bread when I left Auschwitz, and that was gone soon. And you know, it was cold. It was-- we ate snow, and I know, now, that that wasn't such a good idea. But that's the only thing we had as liquid, liquid, you know, because we were, we were completely dehydrated. We arrived in a place, I think it was called [INAUDIBLE] or Breslau. I can't remember for sure. And we were taken to this railroad yard, and we were put into open box cars, these metal cars-- the open ones that carries iron or coal, you know. And we were stuffed in into these cars, and-- just like sardines. And only the heads were sticking out, like a bunch of cabbages, you know. And I think this helped to keep our bodies, at least, warm. But our heads were pretty cold, you know. There was nothing on our ears, you know, and-- and a train was going west. We came into Czechoslovakia. And I remember the train stopped in a town called [INAUDIBLE] at a railway station, and it was early morning. It was a dirty, early morning snow. And it was still dark. I guess it was probably around 7:00 in the morning. There was a snow drizzle. And there was a walkway over the rail where people walk across the railway yard. And as soon as the Czech people saw-- or how they got wind of it, I don't know-- but a lot of Czech people came with these big bakers' breads. And they started to throw bread and buns into the train. Unfortunately, in there, I was too far. I couldn't catch anything. But the Nazis, you know, when we stopped at this station in [INAUDIBLE], they got off the train, and they were patrolling all around the train, so that nobody can run away from this train, you see. And they were yelling at the people not to throw bread, because these are Jews, you see. But the Czechs just kept throwing the bread, you know. And finally the Nazis, these guards, were starting to shoot at these people. And I don't know whether anybody was hurt or not. But you know, I just remember this incident, you know, that these people came, and-- I mean, you know, I hadn't seen kindness like this from anybody, you know. And that was really-- it meant something, to see that people were willing to do something like that, even though I wasn't able to get ahold of any of this bread, you see.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, and so we continued. I remember going to [INAUDIBLE]. This is a big city. And then we finally, we entered Austria, because I remember seeing the signs on the railroad stations. And we arrived in a place called Mauthausen. The train stopped. It was beginning of February of 1945.

speaker: interviewee
text: The train stopped on this side at-- of the Danube River. And I could see the railroad bridge across the Danube, you know, and that's quite a bridge, you know. And I couldn't understand why the train did not go across the bridge. They ordered us to get out of the train. And as soon as you get off, you had to line up into columns. And I thought that they were going to kill us, right here. And I remember looking at the Danube. The water was kind of greenish color, and the ice floe going down towards Hungary. And I said, this is it, you know. But no, they told us to start marching. And we marched across the bridge. I remember going through on those railroad ties. And the rails were damaged. The bridge was damaged from Allied bombing. And I remember going through, and I looked down a few times, and I saw the water, you know. And I was just concentrating that I shouldn't fall through one of these railroad ties to make it to the other side of the bridge. And we got across, and here we come to this nice little city of Mauthausen. And that was really a shock to me, you know, to see this city-- these nice homes, you know, with this filigree work. I remember this so vividly-- and these nice clean curtains, you know, white curtains, on the windows. It was like a picture-type of situation. And we marched in the middle of the road. We were so filthy by then, you know. And we were black from frostbitten. And we must have been quite a sight. And we were marching in the slush in the middle of the road. And I remember so vividly, you know, these Austrian women, they were putting children in sleighs on the sidewalk. You know, children were well-fed with nice hats and scarves. And I can only remember these women looking towards the storefronts or the homes, and nobody wanted to look at us, you know. And I looked at these homes, and I said, wouldn't that be wonderful-- if I could just be there for one day and have a piece of bread and wash myself and lay down in a bed, you know, with some clean sheets. That, that was the only thing I said, you know, I would give my life if I could do this. And so we marched through Mauthausen, and we came to a hill. And on the right side there were these granite quarries where they were cutting granite rock from the mountainside. And I was looking up, and I saw these prisoners with the striped suits and the striped outfits hanging on the side, you know, on ledges, you know, with hammers and chisels, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I just shuddered, you know. I said, what kind of place is this, you know? This was a terrible place. And arrived in the camp.
